items.title: Lithiasic composition
items.contentHtml:
Classification by stone composition

  • Calcium oxalate (monohydrate/dihydrate): more common (~70-80%).
  • Calcium phosphate (brushite, apatite): associated with renal tubular acidosis or hyperparathyroidism.
  • Uric acid (10-15%): radiolucent on plain radiography; Urinary pH <5.5 favors its formation.
  • Struvite (magnesium ammonium phosphate): staghorn stones due to urease infection (Proteus).
  • Cystine (<1%): autosomal recessive inheritance; hexagonal in urine.

items.foco: Hypercalciuria as the most common cause
items.title: Hypercalciuria
items.contentHtml:
Hypercalciuria as the most frequent cause

  • Main metabolic factor in calcium lithiasis: idiopathic hypercalciuria (absorptive, resorptive or renal).
  • Definition: urinary excretion of calcium >300 mg/day in men or >250 mg/day in women, or >4 mg/kg/day.
  • Mechanism: increased renal calcium excretion → urinary supersaturation → nucleation of calcium oxalate/phosphate crystals.
  • Evaluation: calcemia, PTH, calciuria in 24-h urine; rule out primary hyperparathyroidism.

items.foco: Renoureteric colic and its typical irradiation
items.title: Renourethral colic
items.contentHtml:
Renoureteral colic and its typical irradiation

  • Sudden onset, intense pain in the lumbar fossa (costovertebral angle) that radiates to the flank and inguinal region ipsilateral.
  • Irradiation to the testicle (man) or labia majora (woman) innervation of the genitofemoral nerve (L1-L2 roots).
  • As the stone descends, the pain migrates to the bladder and may be accompanied by dysuria or urinary urgency (juxtavesical stone).
  • Differential diagnosis: acute appendicitis (pain in the right iliac fossa without typical inguinal irradiation), ectopic pregnancy, testicular torsion.

items.title: Microscopic hematuria
items.contentHtml:
Microscopic hematuria in urolithiasis

  • Typical finding: >3-5 red blood cells/field in urinary sediment without macroscopic hematuria.
  • Present in ~80-90% of patients with renal colic; its absence does not rule out lithiasis.
  • Mechanism: erosion of the urothelium due to the stone or intraluminal hyperpressure.
  • Trap: isolated hematuria without pain requires ruling out urothelial tumor (cystoscopy, cytology).
  • In lithiasis, hematuria is usually transient and resolves with the expulsion of the stone.

items.foco: UTI without contrast as initial study
items.title: computed tomography without contrast
items.contentHtml:
UTI without contrast as initial study

  • Helical computed tomography without contrast is the test of choice in suspected urolithiasis (sensitivity 95-100%, specificity 96-100%).
  • Detection of radiolucent stones (uric acid, cystine, indinavir).
  • Evaluates size, location (proximal, middle, ureter, distal), density (UH) and indirect signs (hydronephrosis, crown sign [ring of soft tissue around the stone]).
  • No preparation required; Relative contraindication: pregnancy (prefer ultrasound).

items.foco: Size less than 5 mm for spontaneous expulsion
items.title: Spontaneous expulsion
items.contentHtml:
Size less than 5 mm for spontaneous expulsion

  • Calculations ≤5 mm have a high probability of spontaneous expulsion (>68-80% in 4-6 weeks).
  • Calculations >5 mm: probability <50%; >10 mm require intervention in >90%.
  • Negative predictors: proximal location (upper ureter), spicules, complete obstruction.
  • Conservative management: hydration, NSAIDs, tamsulosin if >5 mm distal.
  • Trap: a 4 mm stone in the proximal ureter may not be expelled; reevaluate in 2-4 weeks with imaging.

items.foco: NSAIDs as analgesic treatment of choice
items.title: Analgesia with NSAIDs
items.contentHtml:
NSAIDs as analgesic treatment of choice

  • First line: ketorolac 30 mg intravenously/intramuscular or diclofenac 75 mg intramuscularly/intravenous (single dose or every 8 hours).
  • Mechanism: inhibition of prostaglandins → reduction of intraluminal pressure and urinary flow, reducing ureteral distension.
  • Superior to opioids in analgesic efficacy and fewer adverse effects (nausea, respiratory depression).
  • Contraindications: acute renal failure, active peptic ulcer, allergy to NSAIDs, third trimester pregnancy.
  • Associate antiemetic (metoclopramide 10 mg intravenously) if nausea.

items.title: Tamsulosin
items.contentHtml:
Tamsulosin to facilitate stone expulsion

  • Selective α1A/α1D alpha-blocker: relaxes the smooth muscle of the distal ureter (especially in the juxtavesical ureter).
  • Dose: 0.4 mg/day orally for 4-6 weeks.
  • Indication: stones >5 mm and <10 mm in the ureter. distal, to facilitate expulsion (reduces time and need for procedures).
  • Adverse effects: orthostatic hypotension, retrograde ejaculation (reversible).
  • Not recommended in proximal stones >10 mm or with severe obstruction.

items.title: LEOCh
items.contentHtml:
Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy

  • ESWL (extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy): indicated for kidney stones <20 mm and proximal ureteral stones <10 mm.
  • Contraindications: pregnancy, coagulopathy (INR >1.5), aortic aneurysm in the tract, distal obstruction, infection Active urinary tract.
  • Stone-free rate: ~70-80% for kidney stones <10 mm; minor for stones in the lower calyx.
  • Complications: renal hematoma (1%), obstruction by fragments (Steinstrasse).
  • Trap: do not use in cystine stones (very resistant to shock waves).

items.foco: Indications for ureteroscopy or percutaneous nephrolithotomy
items.title: Ureteroscopy and PCNL
items.contentHtml:
Indications for ureteroscopy or percutaneous nephrolithotomy

  • Ureteroscopy (URS): distal ureteral stones >10 mm, ESWL failure, impacted stones, morbid obesity, pregnancy.
  • Percutaneous nephrolithotomy (PCNL): kidney stones >20 mm, staghorn stones, stones in Inferior calyx with unfavorable anatomy, ESWL/URS failure.
  • First line PCNL in cystine stones >15 mm (due to hardness).
  • Complications: hemorrhage, ureteral perforation, stricture, infection.

items.foco: Cystine stones and autosomal recessive inheritance
items.title: Cystinuria
items.contentHtml:
Cystine stones and autosomal recessive inheritance

  • Cystinuria: defect in the tubular transporter of dibasic amino acids (cystine, ornithine, arginine, lysine) → elevated urinary excretion of cystine.
  • Inheritance: autosomal recessive (SLC3A1 gene or SLC7A9).
  • Radiopaque (sulfur) stones, yellowish appearance, hexagonal in urine.
  • Diagnosis: cystinuria >250 mg/g creatinine or positive cyanide-nitroprusside test.
  • Treatment: hyperhydration, urinary alkalinization (pH >7.5), tiopronin or D-penicillamine.

items.foco: Hyperoxaluria in patients with inflammatory bowel disease
items.title: Enteric hyperoxaluria
items.contentHtml:
Hyperoxaluria in patients with inflammatory bowel disease

  • Mechanism: fat malabsorption → fatty acids bind to intraluminal calcium → free oxalate is absorbed in the colon (increased permeability).
  • Common in inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn's, ulcerative colitis), ileal resection, bypass jejunoileal.
  • Enteric hyperoxaluria → formation of calcium oxalate stones.
  • Treatment: low oxalate and fat diet, oral calcium supplements to chelate oxalate in the intestine, cholestyramine.
  • Trap: do not confuse with primary hyperoxaluria (hepatic enzyme defect, autosomal recessive).
